title: Reupholstering a drop-in seat
subtitle: Strip, pad, cover, and staple a removable chair seat
excerpt: How to reupholster a drop-in chair seat: removing the old fabric, replacing or topping up the foam pad, cutting and tensioning the new top cover, and stapling the underside for a neat finish.
sourceNotes: The Upholsterer's Step-by-Step Handbook (Dorling Kindersley). Collins Complete DIY Manual. Traditional Upholstery Workshop guides.

recipeTools.notes: For tapping staples flush and for removing tacks where the original seat was tacked rather than stapled.

glossaryTerms.definition: A removable upholstered seat pad that sits in a rebate in the chair frame without fixing. Lifted out by pressing up through the holes in the seat rail underside. Also called a slip seat.
A thin layer of polyester wadding wrapped over foam before the top cover is fitted. Softens the foam edge and gives the seat a rounded, upholstered profile.

content.text: lifts free of the chair frame, so the work takes place at the bench rather than with the chair upside down. The job has four parts: stripping the old fabric, checking or replacing the foam pad, cutting and tensioning the new top cover, and stapling the underside.
Cut 150 mm larger than the seat in each direction to allow a full wrap and stapling margin. Upholstery-weight fabric (300 g/m² or above) outlasts dress-weight.

Cut to the exact plan size of the seat base. 50 mm depth at medium density is the standard domestic seat pad. UK regulations require combustion-modified high-resilience (CMHR) foam in domestic upholstery.
A single layer over the foam softens the edge line.
A square of lightweight cotton or black cambric stapled to the underside conceals the stapled edges.

Remove the drop-in seat from the chair by pushing upward through the holes in the seat rail underside, or lifting at a corner. Place it face down on a work surface. Pull out all staples or tacks with a staple lifter or flat screwdriver. Remove the old fabric and any worn wadding. Retain the foam if it is still firm and returns to shape when pressed.
If replacing the foam, cut the new pad to the exact plan size of the seat base and place it centrally on the seat. Wrap a single layer of
over the top and down the sides, trimming to within 20 mm of the underside. The Dacron softens the hard foam edge and helps the top cover sit smoothly.
Step 3, cut and tension the cover

Lay the fabric right-side down on the bench. Centre the seat, foam-side down, on the fabric. Fold the fabric up at the mid-point of each long side and place a single staple to hold the tension while you work. Do not staple corners first.

Pull the fabric firmly over the edge at each mid-point and staple at 50 mm intervals, working from centre outward to 50 mm from each corner. Complete the opposite side before adding more staples to either long side. The fabric must be drum-tight with no wrinkles on the top face.

At each corner, fold the fabric to form a single neat pleat on the underside of the seat. Pull each pleat tight, staple across the fold, and trim any excess fabric with the upholstery shears to reduce bulk. The top face of the corner should be smooth with no tucks or ridges.

Cut a piece of cambric or calico 20 mm smaller on each side than the underside of the seat. Fold a 10 mm hem on each edge, place it centrally on the underside, and staple at 80 mm intervals around the perimeter.

attrs.symptom: Ridges or pleats visible on the top face
attrs.cause: Fabric not pulled evenly from opposite sides, or Dacron wrap bunched at the corners.
attrs.fix: Pull out the staples on the side with the ridge, re-tension firmly from the opposite side, and re-staple. Check the Dacron has not folded over itself.

attrs.cause: Too much excess fabric folded into the corner pleat, or the pleat folded in the wrong direction.
attrs.fix: Remove the corner staples, trim excess fabric, and fold again into a single clean pleat. Fold away from the front rail direction for the neatest appearance from the front.
